teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** English law draws a sharp distinction between pure economic loss and consequential economic loss. **Pure economic loss** is loss that is not consequent upon physical damage to the claimant's person or property — e.g. lost profits, wasted expenditure, or diminution in the value of an asset caused by a third party's negligence. The general exclusionary rule is that pure economic loss is *not* recoverable in the tort of negligence. The leading authority is *Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd* [1973] QB 27.

**Spartan Steel v Martin:** The defendants negligently cut a power cable, causing a power cut to the claimant's factory. The damages fell into three categories: (1) physical damage to metal in the furnace (recoverable — this was consequential loss linked to property damage); (2) lost profit on that damaged metal (recoverable — consequential); and (3) lost profit on the metal that *would have* been processed during the power cut (pure economic loss — NOT recoverable). Lord Denning MR stated that policy reasons — the crushing weight of such claims, the risk of indeterminate liability — justified the exclusionary rule.

**Policy Rationale:** The exclusionary rule prevents 'liability in an indeterminate amount for an indeterminate time to an indeterminate class' (Cardozo CJ in *Ultramares Corp v Touche* (1931), cited in *Spartan Steel*). Unlike physical damage, which is finite and bounded, economic losses can ripple unpredictably through the economy. A single power cut might cause economic losses to hundreds of businesses downstream.

**In short:** No duty of care is owed in negligence to avoid causing pure economic loss. The claimant who suffers only lost profits, without personal injury or property damage, cannot recover.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27
Ultramares Corp v Touche (1931) 255 NY 170

question: _________ Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27 is the leading authority establishing that pure economic loss is not recoverable in the tort of negligence.
feedback: Correct. *Spartan Steel v Martin* [1973] QB 27 — the defendants negligently cut a power cable. The lost profit on unprocessed metal (head 3) was pure economic loss and irrecoverable. Only the physical damage and its direct economic consequences (heads 1 and 2) were recoverable.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: If a claimant suffers neither personal injury nor property damage, the loss is classified as ________ economic loss and is generally irrecoverable in negligence.
feedback: Correct. Pure economic loss is economic loss unconnected to any physical damage to the claimant's person or property. *Spartan Steel* [1973] — head 3 (lost profit on uncirculated metal) was pure economic loss. Head 1 (damaged metal) and head 2 (lost profit on that metal) were consequential.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: In *Spartan Steel* [1973], classify each head of damage as recoverable or irrecoverable:
answer: Physical damage to the metal melt in the furnace at the time of the power cut=Recoverable — consequential (head 1); Lost profit on the sale of that damaged metal=Recoverable — consequential (head 2); Lost profit on four further melts that could not be processed during the 14-hour power cut=Irrecoverable — pure economic loss (head 3)

options.text: Physical damage to the metal melt in the furnace at the time of the power cut
Lost profit on the sale of that damaged metal
Lost profit on four further melts that could not be processed during the 14-hour power cut

feedback: Correct. The key distinction is between loss flowing from physical damage to property (recoverable) and loss flowing merely from the inability to use property or the interruption of business (pure economic loss, irrecoverable).
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: A construction company negligently severs a water main, cutting the water supply to a restaurant for three days. The restaurant suffers no physical damage to its property. It loses £15,000 in revenue from cancelled bookings and £5,000 on food that spoiled because the freezers could not operate. Applying *Spartan Steel* [1973]:

options.text: The restaurant can recover all £20,000 — the loss of water supply was the defendant's fault and all losses flow from it
options.feedback: Incorrect. The exclusionary rule distinguishes between types of loss. Not all losses flowing from negligence are recoverable.

options.text: The restaurant can recover nothing — all loss from a severed water main is pure economic loss
options.feedback: Incorrect. If food spoiled due to the lack of water, that could be property damage (consequential loss). The lost bookings are pure economic loss.

options.text: The restaurant can recover the £5,000 for spoiled food (consequential loss from physical damage to property) but not the £15,000 in lost revenue from cancelled bookings (pure economic loss, analogous to *Spartan Steel* head 3)
options.feedback: Correct. The spoiled food is physical damage to the restaurant's property — recoverable. The lost bookings are purely economic — loss of anticipated revenue with no physical damage to the restaurant's premises. This mirrors *Spartan Steel*.

options.text: The restaurant can recover the £15,000 in lost revenue because it is a foreseeable consequence of the severed main
options.feedback: Incorrect. Foreseeability alone is insufficient. Even though lost revenue is foreseeable, the exclusionary rule for pure economic loss overrides foreseeability. A duty of care is not owed for pure economic loss.

feedback: The distinction between physical damage and pure economic loss is critical. The spoiled food is property damage (even if caused indirectly through loss of utility services). The £15,000 in lost bookings is pure economic loss — no physical damage to the restaurant was required for that loss to occur; it was simply the inability to trade. *Spartan Steel* [1973] — head 2 (consequential economic loss) is recoverable; head 3 (pure economic loss) is not.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question:
Spot the legal error:

'English law permits recovery of all foreseeable economic losses caused by negligence. If a defendant negligently causes a power cut, they must compensate all businesses in the area for all the profits they lost during the outage, because the loss is a foreseeable consequence of cutting the cable.'
answer: Wrong. The exclusionary rule in *Spartan Steel* [1973] prevents recovery of pure economic loss. Foreseeability is not the test. The law deliberately limits liability to physical damage and consequential economic loss; pure economic loss (lost profits without property damage) is irrecoverable.
feedback: Correct. The error assumes foreseeability is sufficient. *Spartan Steel* [1973] QB 27 — Lord Denning was explicit: even though economic loss to many businesses is foreseeable from a power cut, 'the crushing weight of such claims' and the risk of indeterminate liability mean the law draws a line. The exclusionary rule is a policy-based limitation on the duty of care.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: Applying *Spartan Steel* [1973], which TWO losses would be classified as pure economic loss and thus irrecoverable?

options.text: The cost of replacing a factory roof damaged by a negligently operated crane
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is physical damage to property — recoverable as consequential loss. The defendant owes a duty not to cause physical damage.

options.text: Lost sales revenue of a shop that had to close for two days because the negligence of a neighbouring builder blocked access to its entrance
options.feedback: Correct. The shop's premises were not physically damaged — the loss is purely economic (inability to trade). No physical damage to the claimant's property is needed for the loss to arise.

options.text: Lost profit on a cargo of fish that spoiled when a negligently maintained refrigeration unit on a ship failed
options.feedback: The fish spoiling is physical damage to property (the fish). The lost profit on that spoiled cargo is consequential economic loss — recoverable. Distinguish from future catches that were never made — those would be pure economic loss.

options.text: Loss of anticipated profits on a contract that the claimant could not fulfil because a negligently severed cable prevented communication with the client
options.feedback: Correct. No physical damage to the claimant's property or person. The loss arises purely from the inability to use a service or communicate — classic pure economic loss under *Spartan Steel*.

options.text: Medical expenses for a pedestrian injured by debris from a negligently caused explosion
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is consequential economic loss flowing from *personal injury* — always recoverable. Personal injury is the most protected interest in negligence.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). Both are losses unconnected to any physical damage to the claimant's own person or property — they are pure economic losses. (a) is property damage; (c) flows from property damage; (e) flows from personal injury. None of those are 'pure' economic loss.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: In *Spartan Steel* [1973], Lord Denning MR cited the judgment of Cardozo CJ in the US case Ultramares Corp v __________ (1931) on the risk of 'liability in an indeterminate amount for an indeterminate time to an indeterminate class.'
feedback: Correct. *Ultramares Corp v Touche* (1931) 255 NY 170 — Cardozo CJ articulated the floodgates argument in the context of auditor liability. Lord Denning adopted this reasoning in *Spartan Steel* to justify the exclusionary rule for pure economic loss in negligence.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27
Ultramares Corp v Touche (1931) 255 NY 170

question: A ferry operator negligently grounds a vessel, blocking the only shipping channel to a port for three days. A fish processing company cannot receive its daily delivery of fresh fish. The fish that would have been delivered rots on the supplier's dockside. The processing company loses £200,000 in profits from being unable to fulfil orders. Applying *Spartan Steel* [1973] and *Cattle v Stockton Waterworks* (1875):

options.text: The processing company can recover all losses — the ferry operator should have foreseen the impact on businesses dependent on the channel
options.feedback: Incorrect. Foreseeability is not the test. The exclusionary rule applies regardless of whether economic losses are foreseeable.

options.text: The processing company cannot recover — the loss is purely economic (lost profits); the company suffered no physical damage to its own property; the general exclusionary rule applies
options.feedback: Correct. The processing company's loss is classic pure economic loss — lost profits without physical damage to its own property. The rotting fish belongs to the supplier, not the processor. The processor has no proprietary interest in the fish until delivery (*Cattle v Stockton Waterworks* (1875) LR 10 QB 453).

options.text: The processing company can recover for the profits lost in the first 24 hours only — after that, the losses are too remote
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no 'first 24 hours' rule. Remoteness is about the kind of damage, not a time limit. The question is pure vs consequential, not remoteness in time.

options.text: The processing company can recover if it had a contractual right to the fish that spoiled
options.feedback: Even if the risk had passed, the fish on the dockside was likely still the supplier's property. If the processor had title, it could claim for property damage. But on the facts, it likely doesn't — and title in goods is not the same as owning the channel.

feedback: This scenario echoes *Cattle v Stockton Waterworks* (1875) LR 10 QB 453, where a contractor who was delayed by a flooded tunnel could not recover his extra costs from the waterworks company that caused the flood — he had no proprietary interest in the land flooded, and his loss was purely economic. Similarly, the fish processor has no property damage and claims only for lost profits. The exclusionary rule applies.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27
Cattle v Stockton Waterworks Co (1875) LR 10 QB 453

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** The exclusionary rule admits recognised exceptions, the most important of which is the negligent misstatement exception established in *Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd* [1964] AC 465. Two further significant exceptions relate to disappointed beneficiaries (*White v Jones* [1995] 2 AC 207) and negligent references (*Spring v Guardian Assurance plc* [1995] 2 AC 296).

**Hedley Byrne v Heller:** The claimants (advertising agents) asked their bank to inquire into the financial stability of a customer. The customer's bank (Heller & Partners) provided a favourable reference but included a disclaimer of responsibility. The claimants relied on the reference, extended credit, and suffered losses when the customer went into liquidation. The House of Lords held that a duty of care in negligence *could* arise for pure economic loss caused by negligent misstatement — if there was a 'special relationship' between the parties (akin to contract), where the defendant assumed responsibility and the claimant reasonably relied on the statement. However, the disclaimer was effective and Heller was not liable.

**Requirements for Hedley Byrne Liability:** (1) A special relationship between the parties; (2) the defendant possessed special skill or knowledge; (3) the defendant voluntarily assumed responsibility; (4) the claimant reasonably relied on the statement; (5) it was reasonable for the claimant to rely in the circumstances. Disclaimers can negate the assumption of responsibility.

**White v Jones (Disappointed Beneficiaries):** A solicitor negligently delayed preparing a will, and the testator died before it was executed. The intended beneficiaries lost their inheritance. The House of Lords held (by a 3:2 majority) that the solicitor owed a duty of care to the disappointed beneficiaries, even though they were not clients. Lord Goff reasoned that otherwise a 'lacuna' would exist — neither the testator's estate (which had suffered no loss) nor the beneficiaries (who had no contract) could claim. The solicitor assumed responsibility towards the intended beneficiaries.

**Spring v Guardian Assurance:** An employer gave a negligent reference for a former employee, causing the employee to be unable to find work in financial services. The House of Lords held the employer owed a duty of care to the employee in preparing the reference. The case extends Hedley Byrne to the employment context — an employer providing a reference assumes responsibility to the subject of the reference as well as to the recipient.

**In short:** The exceptions to the exclusionary rule all involve an *assumption of responsibility* by the defendant and *reasonable reliance* by the claimant. The ordinary Caparo three-stage test operates alongside the special Hedley Byrne requirements.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
White v Jones [1995] 2 AC 207
Spring v Guardian Assurance plc [1995] 2 AC 296
Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605

question: _________ Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465 established that a duty of care may be owed for pure economic loss caused by a negligent misstatement where there is a 'special relationship' between the parties.
feedback: Correct. *Hedley Byrne v Heller* [1964] AC 465 — the House of Lords recognised for the first time that a duty of care in negligence could arise for pure economic loss caused by words. The key requirements are a special relationship, assumption of responsibility, and reasonable reliance.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: In __________ v Jones [1995] 2 AC 207, the House of Lords held that a solicitor who negligently failed to prepare a will owed a duty of care to the intended beneficiaries, who suffered pure economic loss as a result.
feedback: Correct. *White v Jones* [1995] 2 AC 207 — a 3:2 majority extended the assumption of responsibility principle to disappointed beneficiaries. Lord Goff reasoned that without this exception, there would be a 'lacuna' (gap) in the law — no one could recover.
oscoaReferences: White v Jones [1995] 2 AC 207

question: Match the key requirements to establish liability under the *Hedley Byrne* [1964] exception:
answer: A 'special relationship' between the parties=The relationship is proximate, akin to a contractual relationship; Voluntary assumption of responsibility=The defendant chose to provide the advice or information and undertook responsibility for it; Reasonable reliance by the claimant=The claimant relied on the statement and it was reasonable for them to do so in the circumstances; Special skill or knowledge on the part of the defendant=The defendant held themselves out as possessing expertise in relation to the statement

options.text: The relationship is proximate, akin to a contractual relationship
The defendant chose to provide the advice or information and undertook responsibility for it
The claimant relied on the statement and it was reasonable for them to do so in the circumstances
The defendant held themselves out as possessing expertise in relation to the statement
options.pair: Special skill or knowledge on the part of the defendant

feedback: Correct. These four requirements underpin the *Hedley Byrne* exception. The concept of 'assumption of responsibility' is the organising principle — applying across *Hedley Byrne*, *White v Jones*, and *Spring v Guardian Assurance*.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: A finance director asks the company's auditor (informally, at a dinner party) whether a particular startup is 'a sound investment.' The auditor says, 'Oh, yes, they're solid — I've looked at their books.' The finance director invests £100,000 and loses everything when the startup collapses. The auditor had not actually reviewed the startup's accounts. Applying *Hedley Byrne* [1964], can the finance director recover?

options.text: Yes — the auditor assumed responsibility by providing an opinion; reliance was reasonable because the auditor is a professional
options.feedback: Close, but the context matters. A social setting without the formal indicia of professional engagement may undermine the special relationship and reasonable reliance.

options.text: No — auditors can never be liable for statements made outside formal engagements
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Hedley Byrne* is not limited to formal engagements. But the context is relevant to whether there was a voluntary assumption of responsibility and whether reliance was reasonable.

options.text: No — the loss is pure economic loss and the exclusionary rule applies without exception
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Hedley Byrne* is a recognised exception to the exclusionary rule. The question is whether the criteria for the exception are satisfied.

options.text: Probably not — while the auditor held themselves out as having special knowledge, a casual dinner-party conversation may not create a 'special relationship' or justify reasonable reliance, especially given the absence of any formal assumption of responsibility
options.feedback: Correct. The context strongly suggests this was informal, social chat — not a professional engagement where the auditor voluntarily assumed responsibility towards the listener. A reasonable person would not rely on a dinner-party opinion for a £100,000 investment without further verification.

feedback: The *Hedley Byrne* exception requires more than a casual exchange of an opinion. The defendant must have voluntarily assumed responsibility, and the claimant's reliance must be *reasonable* in all the circumstances. A statement made in a social context, without the formal trappings of professional practice, is unlikely to found a duty of care. Compare *Chaudhry v Prabhakar* [1989] 1 WLR 29 — a friend who gave car-buying advice was liable, but the context was a specific request to rely on their expertise.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
Chaudhry v Prabhakar [1989] 1 WLR 29

question:
Spot the legal error:

'The principle in Hedley Byrne v Heller [1964] means that anyone who gives careless advice in any context — social, professional, or casual — owes a duty of care to anyone who relies on that advice, and must compensate them for any resulting financial loss.'
answer: This overstates the law. *Hedley Byrne* requires a 'special relationship,' voluntary assumption of responsibility, and *reasonable* reliance. Not every careless statement in any context gives rise to liability. Social or casual settings may not satisfy the requirement of a special relationship justifying reasonable reliance.
feedback: Correct. *Hedley Byrne* [1964] AC 465 is an exception, not a blanket rule. The House of Lords was careful to require a relationship 'akin to contract' — the defendant must have assumed responsibility and the claimant's reliance must be reasonable in context. A pub opinion on a stock tip would not satisfy these requirements. *Caparo v Dickman* [1990] further refined the test for auditor liability, requiring the statement to be made for a *purpose* and to a *known recipient*.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605

question: Which TWO of the following are requirements for liability under the *Hedley Byrne* [1964] exception for negligent misstatement?

options.text: There must have been a pre-existing contractual relationship between the parties
options.feedback: Incorrect. The essence of *Hedley Byrne* is that liability arises in *tort*, not contract. The relationship must be 'akin to contract' but need not actually be contractual.

options.text: The defendant must have voluntarily assumed responsibility for the statement
options.feedback: Correct. Assumption of responsibility is the foundational concept. The defendant must have taken on the task and the responsibility for its careful performance.

options.text: The claimant must have reasonably relied on the statement
options.feedback: Correct. Reliance must be both actual and reasonable. The claimant must show they relied, and that a reasonable person in their position would do so.

options.text: The defendant must have received payment for the advice
options.feedback: Incorrect. Liability can arise for gratuitous advice (*Hedley Byrne* itself involved a free bank reference). The question is voluntary assumption, not payment.

options.text: The statement must have been made in writing
options.feedback: Incorrect. Oral statements can give rise to liability under *Hedley Byrne*. The form of the statement is less important than the context, the assumption of responsibility, and the reliance.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). The core of the *Hedley Byrne* exception is the defendant's voluntary assumption of responsibility and the claimant's reasonable reliance. Payment is not required; oral statements can suffice; and no prior contract is necessary — the relationship is 'equivalent to contract.'
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: Mrs Allen, aged 78, instructs her solicitor to prepare a will leaving her cottage to her niece, Chloe. The solicitor delays; Mrs Allen dies before the will is executed. Under the intestacy rules, the cottage passes to a distant cousin. Chloe receives nothing. Applying *White v Jones* [1995]:

options.text: Chloe can sue the solicitor — the solicitor owed a duty of care to the intended beneficiary; the loss is the value of the cottage Chloe would have inherited
options.feedback: Correct. *White v Jones* [1995] is directly on point. The solicitor assumed a responsibility to the intended beneficiary. The loss is the value of the lost inheritance (pure economic loss, but within the exception).

options.text: Chloe cannot sue — she was not the solicitor's client and had no contract with them
options.feedback: Incorrect. *White v Jones* was decided precisely to fill this gap — the solicitor's duty extends beyond the client to intended beneficiaries, despite the absence of privity of contract.

options.text: Only Mrs Allen's estate can sue, but her estate has suffered no loss because she is deceased
options.feedback: Correct about the estate having no loss — this is exactly the 'lacuna' identified by Lord Goff. But the remedy is that Chloe can sue directly, not the estate.

options.text: Chloe can sue, but only for nominal damages because the solicitor's delay was merely an oversight
options.feedback: Incorrect. Damages in a successful *White v Jones* claim are substantial — the value of the lost inheritance. The solicitor's state of mind is not relevant to the measure of damages.

feedback: *White v Jones* [1995] 2 AC 207 is directly on point. Lord Goff identified a 'lacuna' — if the solicitor is not liable to the beneficiary, no one can recover for the solicitor's negligence (the estate has no loss; the beneficiary has no contract). The assumption of responsibility bridges this gap. Chloe's damages are the value of the cottage she would have inherited.
oscoaReferences: White v Jones [1995] 2 AC 207

question: In Spring v Guardian Assurance plc [1995] 2 AC 296, the House of Lords held that an employer providing a reference for a former employee assumes a duty of __________ to the employee and may be liable for pure economic loss caused by a negligent misstatement.
feedback: Correct. *Spring v Guardian Assurance plc* [1995] 2 AC 296 — the employer gave a reference that was so negative the employee could not work in financial services again. The House of Lords held the employer owed a duty of care in tort, extending *Hedley Byrne* to the employment reference context.
oscoaReferences: Spring v Guardian Assurance plc [1995] 2 AC 296

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Pure economic loss is not recoverable for defective products or buildings — the 'complex structure' theory has been rejected. The leading authority is *Murphy v Brentwood District Council* [1991] 1 AC 398, which overruled *Anns v Merton London Borough Council* [1978] AC 728.

**Murphy v Brentwood DC:** A local authority negligently approved defective building plans. The foundations were inadequate, causing cracks in the walls. The claimant sold the house at a loss before repairs were undertaken, and the cost of full repairs exceeded the diminution in value. The House of Lords held the loss was purely economic — the defective building was a single piece of property, and the cost of repairing it was the cost of remedying a defect in the quality of the product. There was no damage to 'other property' — the house was a single unit. The loss was irrecoverable.

**Complex Structure Theory Rejected:** Under the now-overruled *Anns v Merton*, it had been argued that where a building was a 'complex structure,' damage by one part (e.g. defective foundations) to another part (e.g. walls) constituted damage to 'other property,' triggering recovery. The House of Lords in *Murphy* rejected this — a building is treated as a single piece of property. A defect in one part is a defect in the whole, and the loss is purely economic.

**The Current Position:** A builder, architect, or local authority does not owe a duty of care in tort for pure economic loss arising from defective construction. The remedy lies in contract (against the builder/vendor) or under the Defective Premises Act 1972. *Junior Books Ltd v Veitchi Co Ltd* [1983] 1 AC 520, which had allowed recovery for flooring defects, was confined to its special facts (a relationship 'as close as contract' between the nominated sub-contractor and the building owner) and has not been followed since.

**In short:** No duty in tort for pure economic loss from defective buildings. The remedy is contractual or statutory. The Anns 'two-stage test' is dead; the Caparo three-stage test is the governing framework, and the first stage (foreseeability) is not satisfied for pure economic loss in construction cases.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398
Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728
Junior Books Ltd v Veitchi Co Ltd [1983] 1 AC 520
Defective Premises Act 1972

question: _________ v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398 held that a local authority's negligent approval of defective building plans gives rise to pure economic loss only, which is not recoverable. It overruled Anns v Merton LBC [1978].
feedback: Correct. *Murphy v Brentwood DC* [1991] 1 AC 398 — the House of Lords overruled *Anns v Merton* [1978] and rejected the complex structure theory. A defective building is a single unit; the cost of remedying defects is pure economic loss and irrecoverable in tort.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398
Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728

question: The complex structure theory — that damage by one part of a building to another part constitutes damage to 'other property' — was argued under the now-overruled case of _________ v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728.
feedback: Correct. *Anns v Merton LBC* [1978] AC 728 — overruled by *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991]. The 'complex structure theory' posited that a building could be divided into constituent parts. The theory was rejected by the House of Lords in Murphy: a building is a single piece of property.
oscoaReferences: Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728
Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: Place these cases in the evolution of the law on pure economic loss from defective buildings:
answer: Anns v Merton LBC [1978] — local authority liable for negligent inspection of foundations (two-stage test) → Junior Books Ltd v Veitchi Co Ltd [1983] — nominated sub-contractor liable for defective flooring (high-water mark of recovery) → D&F Estates v Church Commissioners [1989] — plasterwork defect in flat: loss is pure economic, no recovery → Murphy v Brentwood DC [1991] — overrules Anns; no duty for pure economic loss from defects

options.text: Anns v Merton LBC [1978] — local authority liable for negligent inspection (two-stage test)
Junior Books Ltd v Veitchi Co Ltd [1983] — nominated sub-contractor liable (high-water mark)
D&F Estates v Church Commissioners [1989] — defect is pure economic loss, no recovery
Murphy v Brentwood DC [1991] — overrules Anns; complex structure theory rejected

feedback: Correct. This timeline shows the rise and fall of recovery for economic loss in construction cases. *Anns* was the high-water mark for claimants; *Murphy* restored the orthodox exclusionary rule. *Junior Books* was confined to its facts and is now effectively a 'case on its own.'
oscoaReferences: Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728
Junior Books Ltd v Veitchi Co Ltd [1983] 1 AC 520
D&F Estates Ltd v Church Commissioners for England [1989] AC 177
Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: A developer builds a block of apartments with defective cladding. The cladding does not comply with fire safety regulations and must be replaced entirely. No fire has occurred and no personal injury or other property damage has been caused. The cost of replacement is £2 million. The developer went into liquidation and the architect was negligently responsible for the specification. Applying *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991]:

options.text: The architect is liable in negligence — the defective cladding threatens personal safety and the cost of replacement is recoverable as the cost of averting a danger
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Murphy* [1991] rejected the argument that the cost of averting a future danger to safety creates a duty of care for pure economic loss. The loss remains economic (the cost of remedying a defect), not physical.

options.text: The architect is liable — the apartment block is a complex structure and the cladding is 'other property' distinct from the building structure
options.feedback: Incorrect. The complex structure theory was rejected in *Murphy* [1991]. A building is treated as a single piece of property. Defective cladding is a defect in the building, not damage to other property.

options.text: The architect is liable under the Defective Premises Act 1972
options.feedback: The Defective Premises Act 1972 may apply (providing a statutory remedy), but the question concerns tort liability. The DPA and tort are separate claims. The question specifically asks about *Murphy v Brentwood*.

options.text: The architect is not liable in tort — the cost of replacing defective cladding is pure economic loss (the cost of bringing the building up to standard), and *Murphy* [1991] confirms no duty of care is owed for such loss
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Murphy v Brentwood DC* [1991], the defective building is a single unit. The loss is the cost of remedying a defect in its quality — pure economic loss. No duty of care arises. The remedy, if any, lies under statute (Defective Premises Act 1972) or in contract.

feedback: *Murphy v Brentwood DC* [1991] 1 AC 398 is clear: the cost of repairing a defect in a building is pure economic loss and irrecoverable in tort. The fact that the defect poses a safety risk does not convert economic loss into physical damage. The House of Lords was concerned that to hold otherwise would 'open up a very wide field of claims' indistinguishable from a warranty of quality — which is properly the domain of contract law, not tort.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question:
Spot the legal error:

'If a building is complex enough — for example, a hospital with separate wings, each built by different contractors — then a defect in one wing that damages another wing is damage to 'other property' and recoverable in negligence under the complex structure theory.'
answer: Wrong. The House of Lords in *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991] rejected the complex structure theory. A building is treated as a single piece of property regardless of its complexity. A defect in one part is a defect in the whole; the cost of remedying it is pure economic loss.
feedback: Got it. The complex structure theory was argued under *Anns v Merton* [1978] but rejected in *Murphy* [1991]. Lord Bridge stated that 'the foundation, the walls, the floors, the ceiling and the roof are all integral parts of the one building' — there is no meaningful distinction between different parts for the purpose of classifying damage. The building is a single chattel.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: Applying *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991], which TWO losses are irrecoverable as pure economic loss?

options.text: A chandelier falls from a defective ceiling and smashes a valuable table — cost of repairing the table
options.feedback: The table is physically damaged by the chandelier — this is property damage to 'other property' (the table is distinct from the ceiling). The loss is consequential, not pure economic loss.

options.text: The cost of underpinning a house because the foundations were negligently laid and the walls have cracked as a result
options.feedback: Correct. The cracking is damage caused by one part of the house (foundations) to another (walls). Under *Murphy*, the house is a single piece of property. The cost of repair is pure economic loss.

options.text: The diminution in the market value of a house caused by the discovery that it was built with defective materials, before any physical damage has occurred
options.feedback: Correct. Diminution in value from a latent defect is classic pure economic loss — the loss is in the quality of the product, not damage to other property. *Murphy* [1991] confirms this is irrecoverable.

options.text: The cost of repairing a car damaged when a negligently maintained traffic light fell onto it
options.feedback: Incorrect. The car is 'other property' vis-à-vis the traffic light. The damage is physical, and the repair cost is consequential loss — recoverable.

options.text: The loss of profit caused by the closure of a factory while a negligently caused fire is extinguished
options.feedback: This is consequential economic loss flowing from physical damage (the fire). It is recoverable under the *Spartan Steel* head 2 logic — it flows from physical damage to property.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). Both represent the cost of remedying defects in the building's own quality — pure economic loss. (a), (d), and (e) all involve damage to distinct 'other property' — the losses are consequential and recoverable. The distinction between physical damage to other property (recoverable) and inherent defects in the product itself (irrecoverable) is the core of *Murphy*.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: A homeowner buys a newly built house. Six months later, the roof leaks badly because of a negligent design error by the architect. The homeowner incurs £15,000 in repairing the roof and £3,000 in replacing a carpet ruined by the rainwater that entered through the leak. Applying *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991]:

options.text: The architect is liable for both the roof repair (£15,000) and the carpet (£3,000) — both flow from the negligence
options.feedback: Incorrect. The roof repair is the cost of remedying a defect — pure economic loss. The carpet, however, is 'other property' — physically damaged by the water.

options.text: The architect is not liable for either — all loss from a defective building is pure economic loss
options.feedback: Incorrect. While the roof repair cost is pure economic loss, the water-damaged carpet is physical damage to distinct 'other property' — the house (defective product) caused damage to the carpet (separate chattel). That damage is recoverable.

options.text: The architect is liable for the £3,000 carpet (physical damage to other property) but not the £15,000 roof repair (cost of remedying the defect itself — pure economic loss)
options.feedback: Correct. This is the precise distinction drawn in *Murphy* [1991]. The cost of fixing the defective product (the roof) is irrecoverable pure economic loss. But where the defective product causes physical damage to *other* property (the carpet), that damage is recoverable. The carpet is distinct from the building.

options.text: The architect is liable for both — the roof leak created a danger to safety, so the full cost is recoverable
options.feedback: Incorrect. The House of Lords in *Murphy* rejected the argument that safety concerns convert economic loss into recoverable damages. The roof repair remains pure economic loss regardless of safety implications.

feedback: The distinction between *damage to the defective product itself* and *damage to other property* is the critical line drawn in *Murphy v Brentwood DC* [1991] 1 AC 398. The architect is not a warrantor of the building's quality — that is for contract law. But when the defective building causes collateral physical damage to distinct property (the carpet, furniture, a neighbouring building, or a person), that consequential damage is recoverable.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] A dredging company negligently cuts an underwater fibre-optic cable, disrupting internet access to 50 businesses in a coastal town for two days. One business, an online retailer, loses £100,000 in sales because its website was down. No physical damage was caused to the retailer's premises or equipment. Under *Spartan Steel* [1973]:

options.text: The retailer can recover — the dredging company should have foreseen economic harm to businesses reliant on the cable
options.feedback: Incorrect. Foreseeability is not the test for pure economic loss. The exclusionary rule overrides foreseeability.

options.text: The retailer cannot recover — the loss is pure economic loss (lost profits with no physical damage to the retailer's property), and the exclusionary rule applies regardless of foreseeability
options.feedback: Correct. This is analogous to *Spartan Steel* [1973] head 3 — loss of profits from inability to operate due to a severed utility. The cable owner may have a claim for property damage, but the downstream businesses who suffer only economic loss have no claim in tort.

options.text: The retailer can recover for the first 24 hours of lost business but not the second day
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no temporal division. The entire loss is pure economic loss and irrecoverable. *Spartan Steel* drew distinctions by *type* of loss, not duration.

options.text: The retailer can recover because internet access is a necessity, unlike the electricity in *Spartan Steel*
options.feedback: Incorrect. The legal principle does not distinguish between types of utility. Whether electricity, water, or internet — a service interruption causing only economic loss is treated the same way.

feedback: This consolidates the exclusionary rule from Block A. *Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin* [1973] QB 27 — the downstream economic consequences of damage to a third party's property (the cable owner's cable) are pure economic losses. The exclusionary rule prevents indeterminate liability to an indeterminate class — here, 50 businesses, all foreseeable but all outside the scope of the tort.
oscoaReferences: Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] The House of Lords in Hedley Byrne v Heller [1964] established that a duty of care can arise for pure economic loss caused by negligent __________, where there is a 'special relationship' and the defendant has assumed responsibility.
feedback: Correct. *Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd* [1964] AC 465 — the exception for negligent misstatement requires a special relationship, voluntary assumption of responsibility, and reasonable reliance. The defendant's disclaimer was effective.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & B] Which TWO of the following are recognised exceptions to the exclusionary rule against recovery of pure economic loss?

options.text: Negligent misstatement where the defendant had a 'special relationship' with the claimant and assumed responsibility
options.feedback: Correct. *Hedley Byrne v Heller* [1964] is the foundational exception — pure economic loss caused by negligent words is recoverable under specific conditions.

options.text: Loss of anticipated profits caused by a power cut lasting more than 12 hours
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is precisely the *Spartan Steel* [1973] scenario — pure economic loss, not recoverable. There is no exception for longer-duration outages.

options.text: Loss of an expected inheritance caused by a solicitor's negligent failure to prepare a will
options.feedback: Correct. *White v Jones* [1995] — the disappointed beneficiary exception. The solicitor's assumption of responsibility extends to the intended beneficiary.

options.text: Loss of profit caused by damage to a third party's property that the claimant was contractually entitled to use
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Cattle v Stockton Waterworks* (1875) — no recovery for economic loss caused by damage to property the claimant does not own. This remains good law under the exclusionary rule.

options.text: The cost of repairing a defective product purchased by the claimant from a manufacturer with whom they have no contractual relationship
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Murphy v Brentwood* [1991] — the cost of remedying defects in the product itself is pure economic loss. No duty is owed by the manufacturer in tort; the remedy lies in contract (or under the Consumer Protection Act 1987 for defective products causing personal injury or property damage).

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The two principal common law exceptions to the exclusionary rule are negligent misstatement (*Hedley Byrne* [1964]) and disappointed beneficiaries (*White v Jones* [1995]). Both involve an assumption of responsibility. The common law has proven unwilling to extend the exceptions further — the exclusionary rule remains the general principle.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
White v Jones [1995] 2 AC 207
Spartan Steel and Alloys Ltd v Martin & Co (Contractors) Ltd [1973] QB 27

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A, B & C] A surveyor negligently undervalues a property at £200,000 when it was worth £250,000. The buyer, relying on the survey, purchases for £250,000 (the vendor knew the true value and did not discount). Separately, the buyer discovers after purchase that the roof contains asbestos installed by a negligent builder, requiring £40,000 to remove. The asbestos has not yet caused any illness. Applying *Hedley Byrne* [1964] and *Murphy* [1991]:

options.text: The buyer can recover from the surveyor for the undervaluation loss and from the builder for the asbestos removal cost — both are exceptions to the exclusionary rule
options.feedback: Incorrect. The asbestos removal is the cost of remedying a *defect* in the building — pure economic loss under *Murphy*. The builder is not liable in tort.

options.text: The buyer cannot recover from either — undervaluation by a surveyor is pure economic loss, and asbestos removal is also pure economic loss
options.feedback: The surveyor's negligent misstatement falls within the *Hedley Byrne* exception. The surveyor had a special relationship with the buyer and assumed responsibility. The asbestos, however, is governed by *Murphy* — pure economic loss from a defect.

options.text: The buyer can recover from the builder for asbestos removal (a health hazard overriding the exclusionary rule) but not from the surveyor (market transactions are too remote)
options.feedback: Incorrect on both counts. *Murphy* rejected the 'health hazard overrides the exclusionary rule' argument. The surveyor is more likely liable under *Hedley Byrne* than the builder under *Murphy*.

options.text: The buyer can claim against the surveyor under the *Hedley Byrne* exception (negligent misstatement, financial loss from reliance on professional advice) but not against the builder (the asbestos removal cost is pure economic loss — cost of repairing a defect — under *Murphy* [1991])
options.feedback: Correct. The surveyor: *Hedley Byrne* exception — professional advice, special relationship, reliance, pure economic loss recoverable. The builder: *Murphy v Brentwood* — cost of remedying a latent defect is pure economic loss, irrecoverable in tort. Different outcomes under different exceptions to the same overarching rule.

feedback: This scenario tests the interplay between the exclusionary rule and its exceptions. The surveyor's liability is the classic *Hedley Byrne* [1964] scenario — a professional providing a service who assumes responsibility and whose negligent advice causes financial loss. The builder's liability is the classic *Murphy* [1991] scenario — a defect in the quality of a building. The asbestos is part of the building; removing it is remedying a defect, not repairing damage to other property. If the asbestos later caused personal injury, a separate claim in negligence (or under the Consumer Protection Act 1987) might lie — but that is a different cause of action.
oscoaReferences: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] In Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991], the House of Lords overruled __________ v Merton London Borough Council [1978] and rejected the complex structure theory.
feedback: Correct. *Anns v Merton LBC* [1978] AC 728 was overruled by *Murphy v Brentwood DC* [1991] 1 AC 398. The Anns two-stage test was replaced by the Caparo three-stage test, and the complex structure theory was rejected. A building is a single piece of property; defects are pure economic loss.
oscoaReferences: Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991] 1 AC 398
Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728
